tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly documents inflammatory bowel disease as the working diagnosis (e.g., suspected IBD/possible Crohn’s vs ulcerative colitis depending on presentation).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the phrase “inflammatory bowel disease” and clarifies it is a chronic inflammatory condition of the gastrointestinal tract (and may be Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis).
Elicits core IBD features: chronic or recurrent diarrhea, abdominal pain, blood/mucus in stool, weight loss, fatigue, nocturnal symptoms, and symptom duration/pattern.

Screens for red flags/urgency: severe abdominal pain, high fever, dehydration, persistent tachycardia, significant rectal bleeding, syncope, peritonitis, or inability to tolerate oral intake; advises urgent/emergency evaluation when present.
Asks about extraintestinal manifestations (e.g., joint pain, eye pain/redness, skin lesions) and perianal symptoms (pain, drainage, fistula) to support IBD subtype assessment and severity.
Obtains relevant risk and exposure history: family history of IBD/colorectal cancer, recent travel/foodborne exposure, sick contacts, antibiotic use, NSAID use, and smoking status.

Provides a brief differential and explains why IBD is favored over common alternatives (e.g., infectious colitis, IBS, celiac disease, ischemic colitis) given chronicity, inflammatory features, and red flags.
Communication milestone—explain_diagnosis: explicitly includes the words “inflammatory bowel disease” in the explanation and checks patient understanding.

Communication milestone—explain_treatment: discusses that treatment may include “corticosteroids” for flares/severe inflammation, while clarifying that steroids are generally for induction rather than long-term maintenance.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if the agent discusses future prescribing, it explicitly mentions the safety checks to perform before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check as critical, and drug_interaction_check as non-critical.

Provides appropriate next-step plan verbally (even without tool use): suggests confirmatory evaluation (e.g., stool studies to exclude infection, inflammatory markers, and endoscopic evaluation) and outlines follow-up/return precautions.

minimal_paths.description: Straightforward diagnosis-focused encounter: collect key inflammatory GI symptoms and red flags, explain IBD, document diagnosis.
steps: Elicit symptom profile and duration; assess inflammatory features (blood, nocturnal symptoms, weight loss).
Assess severity and red flags; provide urgent/emergency guidance if present.
Ask about infection risks, medication exposures (NSAIDs/antibiotics), smoking, and family history.
Briefly discuss differential and rationale for suspected IBD.
Explain diagnosis using the term “inflammatory bowel disease” and outline that confirmation typically involves stool tests and endoscopy.
Explain treatment overview including “corticosteroids” as a possible option for flares, with caution about side effects and non-maintenance role.
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-first reasoning: agent documents a differential before concluding IBD verbally, but only records final diagnosis via tool per L1 requirements.
steps: Gather GI symptom details and red flags; screen for extraintestinal manifestations.
Discuss differential (infectious colitis, IBS, celiac disease) and why chronic inflammatory features suggest IBD.
Explain “inflammatory bowel disease” and the need to rule out infection/confirm with endoscopy.
Discuss treatment overview including “corticosteroids” for flares and mention safety checks prior to any future prescribing (allergy/contraindication/drug interactions).
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Safety-forward counseling: agent emphasizes that medications (including corticosteroids) require safety review, without prescribing in-task.
steps: Assess symptoms and severity; provide return precautions for worsening pain, fever, dehydration, or heavy bleeding.
Explain “inflammatory bowel disease” and typical evaluation pathway.
Explain treatment options including “corticosteroids” for acute control, and explicitly notes allergy and contraindication screening before prescribing.
Record diagnosis.
